div.div:
Matplotlib is a data visualization library built on NumPy arrays
Seaborn provides high-level API on top of Matplotlib. Unlike Matplotlib, seaborn supports Pandas DataFrame

div.div:
MATLAB-style interface:
- This interface is stateful: it keeps track of the “current” figure and axes, which are where all `plt` commands are applied
- Looks simple; Single flow; No variable assignment
- Problem: once the second panel is created, how can we go back and add something to the first?

div.div: 1. figure: Instance of `plt.Figure`. It is a root container containing all the objects such as axes, labels, graphics
2. axes: Instance of `plt.Axes`: A bounding box with axis, title, legend, and plot elements (line, points, area)
3. Axis (with i): A line with ticks (major & minor), tick formatter and label
3. colorBar: It is a separate axes which provide a mapping b/t colors and array-like values. ColorMaps(cmap=, parameter of various plots) are part of `plt.cm` module

div.div:
1. Title of plot
2. Axis labels (axis with i. Do not confuse with axes)
3. Legends: `plt.legend()`
4. Other labels: colorbar label, coutour label
- text b/t '$' character is defined as LATEX typography (Math). E.g. 'km$^2$', 'log$_{10}$', '$\pi$'

div.div:
- While most plt functions translate directly to ax methods (such as plt.plot() → ax.plot(), plt.legend() → ax.legend(), etc.), this is not the case for all commands:
• plt.xlabel() → ax.set_xlabel()
• plt.xlim() → ax.set_xlim()
• plt.title() → ax.set_title()
- In the object-oriented interface, use `ax.set()` to set all properties at once rather than calling these functions individually

div.div:
3 categories of colormaps:
- Sequential colormaps: consist of continuous sequence of colors (e.g., binary or viridis)
- Divergent colormaps: contain two distinct colors, which show positive and negative deviations from a mean (e.g., RdBu or PuOr)
- Qualitative colormaps: these colors have no particular sequence (e.g., rainbow or jet)
